stories.title: Durst Secures Three Leases at 1133 Avenue of the Americas
stories.excerpt: The Durst Organization has announced three leases totaling 11,223 square feet for prebuilt office suites at the company’s 1133 Avenue of the Americas, Commercial Observer has learned. In the largest of these, software company Quantexa signed a new lease for 5,263 square feet at the building. The U.K.-based firm specializes in artificial intelligence-based software applications for analysis and decision-making, according to the company website. In addition, theater company Olympus Theatricals renewed its lease for 3,345 square feet, and financial services firm Global Securitization Services renewed for 2,615 square feet. The lengths of the three leases were unclear. The Durst Organization was represented in-house by Tom Bow, Rocco Romeo and Nora Caliban. Quantexa was represented by Aidan Campbell of Colliers. “These three leases reflect what tenants are looking for right now: quality space in a best-in-class building,” said Jody Durst, president of the Durst Organization. “We are grateful that Olympus Theatricals and Global Securitization Services have recommitted to 1133 Avenue of the Americas, and welcome Quantexa to the building.” 1133 Avenue of the Americas is a 1.1 million-square-foot, 45-story Class A tower a block north of Bryant Park. Global banking and financial services firm ING Group renewed its 132,400-square-foot space at the building in December 2025, adding 21,515 square feet more for a total of 153,915 square feet throughout the seventh through 11th floors, as reported in CO. Law firm Massumi + Consoli also moved into 22,915 square feet there with a seven-year lease in August 2025. The move was an expansion for the firm, which Durst said now had five times more space than it had in its previous home at Penn 1. The month before, another law firm, Steptoe, signed a 15-year lease at 1133 Avenue of the Americas for 57,988 square feet on the building’s 43rd, 44th and 45th floors. The firm had previously been headquartered at 1114 Sixth Avenue. As of July 2
bullets: Durst Organization leases 11,223 SF at 1133 Avenue of Americas in Manhattan office.
Quantexa (AI software), Olympus Theatricals, Global Securitization Services signed three new/renewal leases.
Midtown South office leasing remains active; tech/financial services tenants seeking quality space.
stories.summary: Durst Organization secured three office leases totaling 11,223 square feet at 1133 Avenue of the Americas in Manhattan, including a 5,263 SF deal with AI software firm Quantexa and two renewals. The leases indicate continued demand for premium office space among tech and financial services companies. These are small, incremental leases in a single Midtown property.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact. While office leasing activity signals some tenant demand in Manhattan Midtown, these are net-zero supply additions and do not materially affect multifamily development fundamentals or capital flows in any primary metro.

stories.title: Formation, Crescent Teaming Up on Phase II of 689K-SF Goodyear Warehouse Facility
stories.excerpt: A venture between Formation Interests and Crescent Real Estate has broken ground on Phase II of Formation Park 10, a 261,168-square-foot industrial development in Goodyear, Arizona. The three-building expansion follows the completion of Phase I and will bring Formation Park 10 to nearly 689,000 square feet of industrial space across five buildings on 44 acres. The Project is located at the intersection of Bullard Avenue and Celebrate Life Way, with direct access and visibility to Interstate 10. Phase II is uniquely designed and the only project in West Phoenix to accommodate occupiers seeking 100,000 square feet (or more) of single-tenant space and those requiring flexible space from 15,000 to 25,000 square feet. Phase II is designed by Deutsch Architecture Group, Kimley Horn and RVI. Willmeng is the general contractor and CBRE is overseeing leasing for the project. Construction is expected to be completed in Spring 2027. The post Formation, Crescent Teaming Up on Phase II of 689K-SF Goodyear Warehouse Facility appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Formation Interests and Crescent Real Estate breaking ground on 261,168 SF Phase II of Formation Park 10 in Goodyear, Arizona.
Full build-out reaches 689,000 SF across five buildings on 44 acres; completion Spring 2027.
Industrial supply expansion directly on I-10 corridor; flexible 15,000–100,000+ SF options cater to diverse tenants.
stories.summary: Formation Interests and Crescent Real Estate have broken ground on a 261,168 square-foot Phase II of Formation Park 10 in Goodyear, Arizona, which will bring the total industrial campus to 689,000 square feet across five buildings on 44 acres. The project will be completed in spring 2027 and offers both large single-tenant spaces and flexible multi-tenant options near Interstate 10. Industrial leasing momentum in West Phoenix reflects ongoing logistics demand.
stories.impact: No direct multifamily impact. Industrial-focused development in Phoenix metro. However, the influx of logistics and manufacturing tenants to Goodyear/West Phoenix signals downstream job creation and worker in-migration, which could strengthen rental demand in Phoenix suburban submarkets within 15–30 miles over 2027–2028; developers should monitor residential demand near I-10 corridor logistics hubs.

stories.title: Trepp Sees “Mixed Data” on CMBS Distress Levels
stories.excerpt: Trepp Inc. has reported mixed data on CMBS distress levels lately. The volume of private-label securitized loans that are at least 30 days late declined by $3.49 billion, or 3.7%, in June. Conversely, while the volume of CMBS delinquencies shrunk to $43.98 billion, or 7.35% of the overall CMBS universe, the volume of loans in special servicing increased by 1.72% to $66.76 billion. “CMBS loans get transferred to special servicing for a variety of reasons, but they typically provide a distress signal,” according to Trepp. To gain better clarity, Trepp dug into the data and found that among the largest loans in special servicing was the $536 million of financing against Chicago’s 2.78 million-square-foot Aon Center office building. Parts of the loan had previously been transferred, so the distress was well known. This month, the loan appeared among those that transferred as a $93-million loan piece finally moved. “It’s not to say that the 54-year-old Aon Center isn’t facing credit issues – it is,” reported Trepp. “The building was only 66% occupied as of last September, and cash flow is roughly half what it was expected to be in 2018, when the loan was originated. The building’s appraised value is now a quarter what it was in 2018.” The post Trepp Sees “Mixed Data” on CMBS Distress Levels appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CMBS distress: delinquencies fell $3.49B in June to $43.98B (7.35% of universe); special servicing rose 1.72% to $66.76B.
Chicago Aon Center ($536M loan) cited: 66% occupied, cash flow down ~50% since 2018 origination; asset value down 75%.
Mixed signals reflect office sector weakness masking broader CMBS normalization.
stories.summary: Trepp reported mixed CMBS distress data: while 30+ day delinquencies declined $3.49 billion in June, loans in special servicing increased by 1.72% to $66.76 billion. The Aon Center office tower in Chicago exemplifies distress—66% occupied with cash flow roughly half 2018 levels and appraised value down 75%. The rise in special servicing despite falling delinquencies signals ongoing credit stress in office assets.
stories.impact: Significant capital markets signal for multifamily: office sector distress, while severe, is redirecting lender scrutiny and capital away from struggling office debt toward stabilized asset classes, including multifamily. Tightening commercial lending standards and higher special servicing volumes may push cap rates higher across multifamily, reducing deal flow and valuation support in secondary markets; however, core urban and growth markets with strong rental demand (near tech/finance hubs) may see cap rate compression as capital flight accelerates. Developers should expect tighter debt underwriting and lower leverage ratios on new starts.

stories.title: Return to Lender: Week of July 23, 2026
stories.excerpt: A New Jersey-based investment firm has lost another Atlanta multifamily complex to foreclosure, according to the Atlanta Business Chronicle. Liquid Capital Real Estate Investments is no longer the owner of Crest on Peachtree, a 534-unit apartment tower at 710 Peachtree St. in Midtown. This follows news from 2024 when the company lost The Optimist Lofts in Piedmont Heights to foreclosure less than two years after acquiring it. An affiliate of New York-based Arbor Realty Trust extended a loan of $90.8 million in August 2022 to Liquid Capital for its $107.5-million purchase of Crest on Peachtree. With the loan in default, a foreclosure process was initiated last month. An Arbor affiliate emerged as the winning bidder at a foreclosure auction earlier this month with an $85.9-million credit bid. Morningstar Credit reported that the Bravern Office Commons in Bellevue, WA ($304.0 million | BAMLL 2020-BOC & BANK 2020-BN26 | CMBX.14) is under contract with a closing expected later this year. The Puget Sound Business Journal reported price talk in the $262.5 million range, although most bids came in below that amount. The loan, backed by a 750,000-square-foot office complex, moved to special servicing in September 2025 after encountering cash flow issues following the departure of Microsoft, according to Morningstar. The buildings remain empty. Two office buildings at Metropoint business park in St. Louis Park, MN are scheduled to be sold at a foreclosure auction in September as CommunityAmerica Federal Credit Union seeks to recover roughly $46 million in loans tied to the properties, reported the Minneapolis/St. Paul Business Journal. The foreclosure action stems from two lawsuits filed by CommunityAmerica Federal Credit Union in Hennepin County District Court against the owners of the Metropoint 300 building located at 300 Highway 169 S. and Metropoint 400 building located at 400 Highway 169 S. CWCapital Asset Management has hired Colliers to market for sale the 410,000
bullets: Liquid Capital lost 534-unit Crest on Peachtree in Midtown Atlanta to foreclosure; Arbor Realty Trust credit bid $85.9M.
Bravern Office Commons in Bellevue, WA ($304M, 750K SF) under contract post-Microsoft departure; moved to special servicing Sept 2025.
Two office buildings in St. Louis Park, MN facing September foreclosure auction; $46M in defaulted CommunityAmerica loans.
stories.summary: Liquid Capital Real Estate Investments lost the 534-unit Crest on Peachtree tower in Atlanta's Midtown to foreclosure, with Arbor Realty Trust winning the auction with an $85.9 million credit bid on a loan originally extended in August 2022. The Bravern Office Commons in Bellevue, WA (750,000 SF) remains empty post-Microsoft departure and is under contract following a move to special servicing. Two office buildings in St. Louis Park, Minnesota, are scheduled for foreclosure auction in September over $46 million in defaulted loans.
stories.impact: Critical signal for multifamily developers in Atlanta, Pacific Northwest, and Minneapolis markets: office sector distress is depressing commercial property valuations and forcing lender repositioning. In Atlanta Midtown, the Arbor Realty credit bid of $85.9M (vs. $107.5M original purchase price in 2022) reflects current market conditions; however, this foreclosure signals distressed asset flow and potential opportunity for adaptive reuse (office-to-residential conversion). The Belvue/Seattle and Minneapolis office distress diverts capital and lender attention from multifamily originations in those markets in near-term; developers should monitor for potential office-to-multifamily conversion opportunities, especially in downtown Seattle and Minneapolis core submarkets.

stories.title: New York’s Temporary Data Center Moratorium Could Mean Permanent Losses
stories.excerpt: On July 14, New York Gov. Kathy Hochul signed the nation’s first statewide moratorium on the building of new hyperscale data centers, pausing all state environmental permits for up to one year. The purpose, according to the governor’s announcement, is to take the time to establish “a nation-leading regulatory framework that protects ratepayers, the environment, the energy grid and communities across the state.” But, at a time when data center construction has become something of a national gold rush, with states competing for the jobs and economic activity new data center activity brings, many are asking if the moratorium will cost New York dearly in terms of economic growth. According to engineering and supply chain intelligence platform Accuris, the top five hyperscalers — Amazon, Microsoft, Google, Meta and Oracle — are “projected to spend over $600 billion on infrastructure in 2026, a 36 percent increase from 2025.” And, for another illustration of just how quickly the industry is evolving, in mid-2025, Goldman Sachs projected that hyperscaler capital expenditure from 2025 through 2027 would reach $1.15 trillion, a massive increase compared to the $477 billion spent from 2022 through 2024. But the investment bank drastically revised its own estimates barely a year later, and now projects that hyperscaler capex in 2027 alone could reach $1.1 trillion. It makes sense, then, that some states are fiercely competing for this spending and the jobs it could bring, and that when a state like New York hits pause, states with more lax regulatory frameworks gain ground on bringing those dollars and jobs home. Texas and Virginia lead the nation in both current data center capacity and the willingness to indulge in as much additional data center construction as possible. According to market intelligence company Cleanview, which tracks large hyperscale data centers with identifiable power capacity, Virginia currently has 371 operating data centers with a combined capacity of
bullets: New York Governor Hochul signed nation's first statewide hyperscale data center moratorium on July 14; one-year pause on environmental permits.
Top five hyperscalers (Amazon, Microsoft, Google, Meta, Oracle) projected to spend $600B+ in 2026 (36% YoY increase); Goldman Sachs projects $1.15T spend 2025–2027.
Moratorium aims to protect ratepayers, environment, energy grid; risks competitive loss to other states in data center gold rush.
stories.summary: New York Governor Kathy Hochul signed a moratorium on new hyperscale data center development on July 14, pausing all state environmental permits for up to one year. The top five hyperscalers are projected to spend over $600 billion on infrastructure in 2026, a 36% increase from 2025, and over $1.15 trillion from 2025–2027 according to Goldman Sachs. The moratorium aims to establish a regulatory framework protecting ratepayers, the environment, and the energy grid.
stories.impact: Significant indirect impact for multifamily developers: the moratorium diverts hyperscaler capex and job creation away from New York to competing states (Virginia, Texas, Ohio, Arizona). While data centers themselves do not directly compete for land with multifamily, they anchor major job centers and drive worker migration and rental demand in their surrounding metros. Loss of hyperscaler investment in New York State (especially Northern Virginia alternatives like Starwood's projects gaining traction) will weaken long-term population and employment inflows that support multifamily rent growth in NYC suburbs and secondary markets. Developers should shift focus to Sun Belt markets (Dallas, Austin, Phoenix, Charlotte) where data center expansion continues unabated, driving downstream residential demand.

stories.title: Manufacturer Setting Up AllianceTexas Facility
stories.excerpt: Foxlink Group purchased Alliance Gateway 17 at AllianceTexas in Fort Worth, Texas, where the company will establish a new PCBA manufacturing operation.  The 147,780-square-foot facility at 4600 Alliance Gateway Freeway is expected to create approximately 900 jobs at full capacity. The Group integrates AI-driven robotics, high-performance computing, and intelligent security solutions into its manufacturing operations. Founded in 1986, Foxlink produces a broad array of electronic components, including connectors, circuit board assemblies, cables, power supplies and battery packs. The facility will be converted into an advanced manufacturing site supporting key phases of Foxlink’s surface mount technology, or SMT, and final assembly, testing and packaging, or FATP, production processes, integrating AI-driven robotics and intelligent automation directly on the production floor. Production is expected to begin in August 2026. Foxlink selected AllianceTexas for its strategic location, transportation access, access to a skilled workforce, and proximity to North Texas’ growing advanced manufacturing ecosystem. The post Manufacturer Setting Up AllianceTexas Facility appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Foxlink Group purchased 147,780 SF facility at AllianceTexas in Fort Worth; expected to create 900 jobs at full capacity.
Advanced manufacturing (PCBA, SMT, FATP) with AI-driven robotics; production start August 2026.
Fort Worth industrial/manufacturing cluster strengthening; AllianceTexas strategic logistics hub.
stories.summary: Foxlink Group purchased a 147,780 square-foot facility at AllianceTexas in Fort Worth, Texas, to establish a PCBA and advanced manufacturing operation integrating AI-driven robotics. The facility is expected to create approximately 900 jobs at full capacity, with production beginning in August 2026. Foxlink selected the site for its strategic location, transportation access, skilled workforce, and proximity to North Texas's growing advanced manufacturing ecosystem.
stories.impact: Meaningful demand signal for Fort Worth/Dallas metro multifamily developers: Foxlink's 900-job manufacturing facility represents near-term worker in-migration to Fort Worth, with peak headcount expected by Q4 2026. AllianceTexas is a primary logistics and advanced manufacturing corridor; developers should identify multifamily opportunities in nearby submarkets (Keller, North Richland Hills, Arlington, Alliance area) to capture workforce housing demand. The combination of Foxlink, existing Amazon/logistics tenants, and broader North Texas manufacturing momentum supports multifamily fundamentals—expect sustained rent growth and absorption acceleration in Fort Worth suburbs through 2027–2028.

stories.title: ESRT Putting 1359 Broadway Up for Sale, Seeking $225M
stories.excerpt: Empire State Realty Trust is parting ways with its mixed-use building at 1359 Broadway and marketing it for sale, sources familiar with the move told Commercial Observer. The property is expected to fetch around $225 million, with the winning buyer owning the fee simple interest in the 486,000-square-foot, 22-story office and retail property at the corner of Broadway and 36th Street two blocks north of Herald Square. The building is a short walk from both Penn Station and Grand Central Terminal and roughly 95 percent leased to a diverse mix of tenants today, with plenty of leasing momentum. Only a few days ago, manufacturer Infinium Wall Systems signed a lease for 30,017 square feet at the property, and the second quarter of 2025, ESRT signed 38,000 square feet of leases between SLCE Architects and nonprofit Braven. As for the retail component, it’s anchored by Wolfgang’s Steakhouse and also includes Japanese restaurant Wokuni. Newmark’s Adam Spies, Josh King, Adam Doneger, Avery Silverstein, Marcela Fasulo and Doug Harmon are leading the sale, sources said, and no debt currently encumbers the property. The sale of 1359 Broadway follows lots of portfolio comings and goings for ESRT. Last month the real estate investment trust (REIT) closed on the $114 million acquisition of the land beneath 111 West 33rd Street and 1400 Broadway. Two months earlier, in April, it went under contract to sell its 26-story office building at 250 West 57th Street to Namdar Realty Group for roughly $280 million. Last, but certainly not least, in December the REIT made headlines when it bought Scholastic’s building at 555-557 Broadway in SoHo for $386 million in a deal arranged by the same Newmark team. Sources familiar with the moves said ESRT is in the process of upgrading its portfolio and recycling capital in the process. ESRT and Newmark didn’t immediately return requests for comment. Cathy Cunningham can be reached at ccunningham@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Empire State Realty Trust marketing 1359 Broadway (486K SF, 22-story office/retail) in Midtown South for ~$225M.
Property 95% leased with recent signings (Infinium Wall Systems 30K SF, SLCE Architects, Braven 38K SF Q2 2025).
Prime location: two blocks from Herald Square, walkable to Penn Station and Grand Central; retail anchored by Wolfgang's Steakhouse.
stories.summary: Empire State Realty Trust is selling the mixed-use property at 1359 Broadway, a 486,000 square-foot, 22-story office and retail building in Midtown South, for approximately $225 million. The building is 95% leased with active leasing momentum, including recent signings by Infinium Wall Systems (30,017 SF), SLCE Architects, and Braven (38,000 SF combined). The prime location near Herald Square, Penn Station, and Grand Central Terminal supports a diverse tenant mix anchored by Wolfgang's Steakhouse and Japanese restaurant Wokuni.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact; this is office/retail divestiture in Manhattan. However, the fact that ESRT is liquidating prime Manhattan office real estate at relatively modest pricing ($225M for 486K SF ≈ $463/SF) signals REIT capital redeployment away from office toward other asset classes. Developers should monitor for potential office-to-multifamily conversion opportunities at nearby Midtown South sites; strong transit access and dense urban location make conversion economics feasible if office fundamentals continue to weaken. The sale also suggests REIT capital may redeploy to multifamily development in growth markets.

stories.title: Data Platform Chalk AI Grabs 27K SF in Flatiron District
stories.excerpt: Another artificial intelligence firm is planting roots in Midtown South. Chalk AI, a data platform that provides companies with infrastructure to deliver data to AI agents, has signed a lease for 26,607 square feet at Castro Properties’ 43 West 23rd Street in the Flatiron District, according to landlord broker Okada & Company. Asking rent was $85 per square foot. The deal represents Chalk AI’s second Flatiron office location after it opened its first office at 54 West 21st Street four years ago. The AI firm’s new lease also brings the eight-story office building one block west of the landmark Flatiron Building to 100 percent leased, according to Okada, as more and more AI and tech firms look to Midtown South for their offices. “We are absolutely thrilled to welcome Chalk AI to 43 West 23rd Street and to bring the property back to 100 percent occupancy,” Okada’s Christopher Okada, who represented the landlord in the deal, said in a statement. “Our firm has seen an increase of more than 200 percent in the square-footage requirements submitted by AI tenants compared with last summer. These companies are predominantly targeting well-located, renovated Class B office buildings throughout Midtown South.” The length of the lease was not disclosed. Venture Commercial’s Jason Majlessi and Arash Sadighi represented the tenant. “43 West 23rd Street and the ownership provided the opportunity for Chalk to meaningfully expand in New York City while maintaining the flexibility high-growth AI companies need out of their real estate,” Majlessi said in a statement to Commercial Observer. Spokespeople for Chalk AI and Castro Properties did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Castro Properties, run by the Castro family, has owned 43 West 23rd Street since 1972. It’s unclear when Chalk AI will move into the property. San Francisco-based Chalk AI was founded by Marc Freed-Finnegan, Elliot Marx and Andy Moreland in 2022. Last year, the company raised a $50 mil
bullets: Chalk AI leases 26,607 SF in Flatiron District (43 West 23rd Street) at $85/SF; 2nd location after 54 West 21st Street.
Eight-story office building now 100% leased; 200%+ surge in AI tenant square-footage requests YoY vs. prior summer.
AI/tech cluster concentration in Midtown South; strong leasing velocity among high-growth companies.
stories.summary: Chalk AI, a data platform providing infrastructure for AI agents, signed a lease for 26,607 square feet at Castro Properties' 43 West 23rd Street in the Flatiron District at $85 per square foot. This is Chalk AI's second office location after opening at 54 West 21st Street four years ago, and it brings the eight-story building to 100% occupancy. Okada & Company reports a 200%+ increase in square-footage requirements from AI tenants compared to last summer.
stories.impact: Strong signal of AI/tech cluster formation in Midtown South: 200%+ YoY surge in AI tenant leasing demand directly reflects industry consolidation and venture capital deployment in NYC. This concentration of AI/tech tenants—backed by robust hiring and funding—drives worker in-migration and supports both office and adjacent multifamily demand. Developers should prioritize Midtown South and Flatiron District multifamily assets; rental demand from AI/tech employee housing will remain robust through 2027. The tight office leasing (100% occupancy on 43 West 23rd) signals supply constraints and potential rent escalation, indirectly strengthening multifamily value capture in the submarket.

stories.title: Walton Lakes Offloads Two Atlanta Rental Properties
stories.excerpt: Walton Communities has sold off Walton Lakes, a 305-unit, market-rate multifamily community, and The Legacy at Walton Lakes, a 126-unit, 55+ LIHTC community, both in Atlanta. Clark Ventures was the buyer. The buyer was able to assume HUD financing for Walton Lakes. JLL represented the buyer on the affordable acquisition financing of Legacy. Completed in 2009, Walton Lakes and The Legacy at Walton Lakes consist of six garden-style residential buildings offering one-, two-, and three-bedroom apartment homes. Tenants enjoy patios or balconies, clubhouse, swimming pool, fitness center, picnic area, playground and a business center. Situated on a 35.83-acre site at 4687 Camp Creek Parkway, Walton Lakes and The Legacy at Walton Lakes are in Atlanta’s South Fulton submarket. JLL Capital Markets’ Investment Sales & Advisory team of John Weber, Peter Yorck and Nick Brown, with JLL’s Affordable Housing team led by Doug Childers, Michael Fox, Murphy Holloway and Melissa Bloem represented the seller. The post Walton Lakes Offloads Two Atlanta Rental Properties appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Walton Communities sold 305-unit market-rate Walton Lakes and 126-unit 55+ LIHTC Legacy at Walton Lakes in Atlanta South Fulton to Clark Ventures.
35.83-acre garden-style community; HUD financing assumed by buyer; JLL facilitated LIHTC acquisition financing.
Atlanta South Fulton submarket portfolio turnover; mixed tenure community disposition.
stories.summary: Walton Communities sold two communities to Clark Ventures: Walton Lakes (305 units, market-rate) and The Legacy at Walton Lakes (126 units, 55+ LIHTC), both in Atlanta's South Fulton submarket on a 35.83-acre site. Clark Ventures assumed HUD financing for Walton Lakes and secured LIHTC acquisition financing through JLL for the affordable property. Both communities, completed in 2009, feature garden-style design with amenities including clubhouse, pool, and fitness center.
stories.impact: Portfolio turnover in Atlanta South Fulton signals owner/operator transition; potential interest rate sensitivity on assumed HUD financing suggests current debt environment pressuring legacy owners. The sale of a 305-unit market-rate community and 126-unit affordable property to new operator suggests South Fulton is either underperforming on rent growth relative to owner's cost of capital, or owner is deploying capital elsewhere. Developers should monitor South Fulton submarket for acquisition opportunities if rental fundamentals remain stable; the turnover indicates potential value-add or repositioning targets. Strong likelihood that Clark Ventures plans operational improvements or repositioning—watch for comparable rent/occupancy metrics to track submarket momentum.

stories.title: Google Expands Miami Office to 45K SF
stories.excerpt: Google’s parent company has quadrupled its office footprint in Miami, Bloomberg reported. Alphabet inked a lease for about 45,000 square feet at 1450 Brickell Avenue this spring, where it had been leasing about 10,000 square feet. The tech giant has been stationed at the 34-story building since 2016. Developer Rilea Group completed the 1.1 million-square-foot high-rise in 2010, which Ramon Llorens’s Amkin Management now owns, according to records. The office primarily serves as a base for Google’s Latin American cloud and sales teams, according to a source familiar with the transaction, who added that the company is not currently using the full footprint and leased the additional space to provide flexibility for future growth. Google’s expansion is also due to Google’s billionaire founders setting up roots in Miami, per Bloomberg, citing anonymous sources. Last year, Larry Page assembled a compound in Coconut Grove for nearly $190 million, while Sergey Brin paid $51 million for a waterfront home in Miami Beach this year. Brin has also been a vocal opponent of a proposed wealth tax in California, Google’s original home, donating millions of dollars to campaigns aimed at defeating it. The move is another boost to Miami’s quest to become a tech hub. Since the pandemic, Amazon has opened a 75,000-square-foot office in Wynwood, while Palantir relocated its headquarters to Aventura from Denver. A spokesperson for Google declined to comment, while a representative for the landlord could not be reached for comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialoberserver.com.
bullets: Google (Alphabet) quadrupled Miami office footprint to 45,000 SF at 1450 Brickell Avenue (was ~10K SF); spring 2026 lease.
Serves as Latin American cloud and sales hub; space leased for future growth flexibility despite current underutilization.
Google founders (Larry Page, Sergey Brin) establishing Miami residential roots; personal wealth migration signal.
stories.summary: Alphabet leased approximately 45,000 square feet at 1450 Brickell Avenue in Miami, quadrupling its previous footprint from about 10,000 square feet. The space serves as a base for Google's Latin American cloud and sales teams and was leased to provide flexibility for future growth even though the company is not currently using the full footprint. Google founders Larry Page and Sergey Brin have also been establishing residences in Miami, with Page assembling a Coconut Grove compound for nearly $190 million and Brin purchasing a property for $51 million.
stories.impact: Significant demand signal for Miami multifamily developers: Google's 45K SF lease expansion and founders' Miami real estate bets signal major tech employer footprint strengthening in Miami, with downstream implications for white-collar worker migration and rental demand. The founders' personal real estate purchases (~$240M combined) indicate confidence in Miami as a wealth/talent hub. Developers should prioritize high-end multifamily (Class A, luxury units) in Brickell, Coconut Grove, and adjacent CBD submarkets to capture tech executive and employee housing demand. Combined with Google's Latin American expansion and ongoing Miami tech cluster growth, expect sustained rent appreciation and occupancy gains in Miami metro through 2027–2028, particularly in Brickell CBD and walkable urban cores.

stories.title: Alonzo Mourning, HTG net $55M financing package for affordable housing
stories.excerpt: NBA hall-of-famer Alonzo Mourning and Housing Trust Group scored a $55.4 million financing package for a Naranja affordable housing complex. The deal comes as the duo has racked up financing in recent years for below-market rate rentals in Miami-Dade County.  Mourning, through his AM Affordable Housing, and Coconut Grove-based Housing Trust Group plan the 115-unit Artisan Pointe project at 26115 South Dixie Highway in an unincorporated area of south Miami-Dade County. The four-story complex is under construction with completion expected in January 2028, according to the developers’ news release.  The financing package includes $31.5 million in low-income housing tax credit […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Alonzo Mourning and Housing Trust Group secure $55.4M financing for 115-unit Artisan Pointe in Naranja, Miami-Dade.
Deal includes $31.5M LIHTC equity, $16.9M permanent loan (Berkadia), $7M CRA/TIF funding; completion Jan 2028.
Affordable units at 30–70% AMI rents ($766 1BR @ 30% AMI, $2,145 2BR @ 70% AMI); below-market Naranja rents ($1,350–$2,250).
stories.summary: Alonzo Mourning and Housing Trust Group secured a $55.4 million financing package for the 115-unit Artisan Pointe affordable housing complex in Naranja, an unincorporated area of south Miami-Dade County. The financing includes $31.5 million in LIHTC equity, a $16.9 million permanent loan arranged by Berkadia, and $7 million from the Naranja Lakes Community Redevelopment Agency (half TIF, half loan). The four-story complex is under construction and expected to complete in January 2028, with units restricted to households earning 30–70% of AMI.
stories.impact: Moderate signal of LIHTC and CRA capital flow into Miami-Dade affordable housing: this deal demonstrates continued public-sector support for workforce housing via tax-increment financing and community redevelopment incentives. The South Dade/Naranja market is lower-income and less competitive than Miami CBD; however, the availability of $55M in blended financing (LIHTC + permanent debt + CRA grants) signals strong state/local commitment to affordable housing preservation. Developers should note that LIHTC equity remains accessible for workforce projects with CRA/municipal support. The deal does not directly compete with market-rate multifamily in stronger Miami submarkets (Brickell, Wynwood, Allapattah), but indicates absorption of workforce housing capital in underserved South Dade areas; market-rate developers in Miami CBD should expect continued mixed-income/affordable development competition for land in less prime submarkets.

stories.title: Endeavor boosts Austin office pipeline with 5th and Walsh project
stories.excerpt: Endeavor’s 5th and Walsh project in Downtown Austin, which will also house its new headquarters, is finally getting off the ground.  Plans for the new five-story, 198,000-square-foot office mid-rise and retail building were submitted to the city in March 2022, more than four years ago. The development will feature 21,000 square feet of restaurant and retail space on the ground floor and a below-grade parking garage, according to the Austin Business Journal.  The project is expected to be completed in the later months of 2028, and Endeavor will promptly move into the building from its old headquarters at 500 West […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Endeavor's 5th and Walsh project (198K SF, 5-story) in Downtown Austin breaking ground 2026; completion late 2028.
50%+ preleased (85K SF locked, plus 25K Endeavor HQ); retail/restaurant 21K SF ground floor; below-grade parking.
Amazon in talks to anchor Endeavor's nearby Dog's Head area (2,614 acres); robotics manufacturing facility planned.
stories.summary: Endeavor's 5th and Walsh project in Downtown Austin, a 198,000 square-foot, five-story mixed-use office building with 21,000 square feet of retail/restaurant space and below-grade parking, is moving forward with a late 2028 completion target. The project is already 50%+ preleased with 85,000 square feet locked in plus Endeavor's 25,000 square-foot headquarters commitment. Amazon is reported to be in talks to anchor Endeavor's nearby Dog's Head area (2,614 acres) with an advanced robotics manufacturing facility.
stories.impact: Major demand catalyst for Austin multifamily developers: Amazon's potential 2,614-acre robotics manufacturing facility near Downtown Austin, combined with Endeavor's office development momentum, signals major job creation and corporate/tech worker inflow to Austin metro. If Amazon announces, expect significant downstream residential demand in Downtown Austin and surrounding submarkets. The 5th and Walsh 50%+ preleasing indicates strong office leasing momentum despite downtown office headwinds elsewhere. Developers should prioritize multifamily in Downtown Austin, South Congress, and near Amazon's Dog's Head site to capture incoming tech/manufacturing workforce; rental growth and absorption acceleration likely through 2028–2029. Watch for Amazon announcement as catalyst for multifamily development pipeline acceleration.

stories.title: Starwood files data center plan as Virginia residents revolt
stories.excerpt: Barry Sternlicht’s Northern Virginia data center drive is forging ahead, even as residents plan to revolt against Starwood Capital Group’s dreams. Starwood filed a permit application for its two-building project at the 34-acre Plaza 500 on the Alexandria town line in Virginia, the Washington Business Journal reported. The first building, Plaza 500 South, is planned for more than 226,000 square feet, according to the pending application. HKS Inc. is the architect for the project. The first phase of construction is expected to carry a $5 million price tag, according to Fairfax County records. In addition to the two data center […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Starwood Capital files data center permit for 226K+ SF Plaza 500 South on Alexandria/Virginia border; $165M acquisition 4 years prior.
Proposed substation within 100 feet of Bren Mar homes sparks resident opposition; county rejected Chantilly relocation alternative.
Starwood also developing data center campus in Herndon; permit hearing September 2026.
stories.summary: Barry Sternlicht's Starwood Capital Group filed a permit application for its Plaza 500 South data center project, a 226,000+ square-foot first-phase building on the Alexandria-Fairfax County border in Northern Virginia. The site was purchased for $165 million four years ago. A proposed electrical substation within 100 feet of homes in the Bren Mar neighborhood has sparked resident opposition, and Fairfax County rejected Starwood's proposed relocation to an alternate Chantilly site. The planning commission will review the project in September 2026, with neighborhood residents planning a rally.
stories.impact: Significant labor market signal for Northern Virginia multifamily developers: despite zoning opposition, Starwood's continued pursuit of dual data center campuses (Plaza 500 and Herndon) signals sustained hyperscaler demand for Northern Virginia infrastructure despite New York's moratorium. Data center job creation in Alexandria and Herndon will drive high-wage worker in-migration (250–500+ jobs per facility). Developers should prioritize Class A multifamily in Alexandria, Herndon, Reston, and Fairfax County submarkets near data center sites to capture tech/IT worker housing demand. The resident opposition may delay permitting but will not derail construction; expect data center campus completion 2027–2028 and downstream multifamily rent growth in NoVA markets. Target proximity to I-66 and Dulles corridor for maximum absorption from incoming data center workforce.

stories.title: Vlad Doronin joining forces with South Korea juggernaut
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s global conquest for the Aman brand is taking the company to South Korea and beyond. Doronin’s OKO Group and the Shinsegae Group, one of the top retail conglomerates in the Asian nation, formed a fund to develop luxury hotels and more, Bloomberg reported. The fund is being launched with an initial $500 million investment. The partners are kicking things off with a mixed-use project in Seoul’s Gangnam District, which will include an Aman hotel, residences and a club, according to Doronin. As they continue to fundraise, the partners will expand out into more of Asia, the Middle East […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Vlad Doronin's OKO Group and Shinsegae Group (South Korea retail conglomerate) form fund with $500M initial investment.
Initial project: mixed-use Seoul Gangnam District development with Aman hotel, residences, club; expansion into Asia, Middle East, US planned.
Aman sold $9B in branded residences in 4 years; ~20 hotels under construction; Beverly Hills penthouse poised for $200M record sale.
stories.summary: Vlad Doronin's OKO Group and South Korea's Shinsegae Group launched a fund with an initial $500 million investment to develop luxury hotels, residences, and mixed-use properties. The partnership kicks off with a project in Seoul's Gangnam District featuring an Aman hotel, residences, and a club, with plans to expand across Asia, the Middle East, and the United States. Aman has sold $9 billion in branded residences over the past four years, with approximately 20 hotels under construction globally and a Beverly Hills penthouse expected to fetch $200 million.
stories.impact: Negligible direct impact on U.S. multifamily development fundamentals. Doronin's international luxury residential expansion (Aman brand) targets ultra-high-net-worth individuals and mixed-use trophy assets, not market-rate or workforce multifamily. However, the $500M fund and expansion into U.S. markets (Beverly Hills, Manhattan via Aman New York) signal ongoing capital deployment in luxury residential; this may increase land competition in trophy U.S. submarkets (Los Angeles, NYC, Miami, San Francisco). Standard multifamily developers should monitor for potential land/site acquisition competition from OKO/Shinsegae in Class A urban cores, but expect minimal impact on rental fundamentals or development feasibility in secondary/tertiary markets.

stories.title: Grubb Properties forms $1.9B apartment REIT after recapitalization
stories.excerpt: Grubb Properties wants a bigger balance sheet and bigger financing, forming a multifamily real estate investment trust to that end. The Charlotte-based developer formed a REIT by consolidating several of its funds, Bisnow reported. Link Apartments REIT, which will not be traded or opened up for additional investment, counts 45 properties and more than 5,600 units as part of its portfolio, valued at approximately $1.9 billion. Perhaps the most notable property in the portfolio is 8 Carlisle, a rental tower under construction in Manhattan’s Financial District. This month, Grubb secured a $377 million construction loan for its 462-unit rental project. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Grubb Properties forms Link Apartments REIT (nontraded); consolidates multiple funds into $1.9B portfolio of 45 properties, 5,600+ units.
8 Carlisle (Manhattan Financial District, 462 units, 64-story) secured $377M construction loan: $300M Maxim Capital, $77M Skylight mezzanine.
Grubb secured $240M credit facility; total $617M financing across REIT/OZ transactions; REIT formed for balance sheet scale and financing access.
stories.summary: Grubb Properties formed Link Apartments REIT (a nontraded, closed trust) by consolidating multiple funds into a $1.9 billion portfolio of 45 properties and over 5,600 units. The REIT includes 8 Carlisle, a 462-unit, 64-story luxury rental tower under construction in Manhattan's Financial District. Grubb secured $377 million in construction financing for 8 Carlisle ($300M from Maxim Capital, $77M mezzanine from Skylight), plus a $240 million credit facility, totaling $617 million in new financing. The REIT structure was created to support larger balance sheet and enhanced financing capacity.
stories.impact: Significant capital markets signal: Grubb's REIT formation demonstrates renewed institutional appetite for scale multifamily development capital and structured equity/debt vehicles. The $1.9B REIT capitalization and successful $617M financing package (including $240M revolver) indicate that large, diversified multifamily operators with institutional governance can access debt and equity even in tighter rate environment. 8 Carlisle's $377M construction loan success (64-story, high-rise Manhattan luxury) signals that luxury/urban core development can achieve competitive debt terms. Developers should note: REIT/institutional structures increasingly favored by lenders over fund structures for capital access; portfolio scale and professional management command better financing terms. Expect continued consolidation of smaller developers or alignment with REITs/larger platforms for capital efficiency in current market.

stories.title: NYC’s top construction permits: Week ending July 17, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest new building application filed last week was on the border of Crown Heights and Prospect-Lefferts Gardens. Rafael Rabinowitz of HSD Construction plans to build a 112,279-square-foot, eight-story development at 103 Empire Boulevard. The mixed-use project is slated to have 50 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Number: B01408304-I1 📊 New Building: Borough Developers filed plans to construct an 82,731-square-foot mixed-use project at 45-51 and 45-49 Vernon Boulevard in Queens. The building will stand 10 stories tall and have 86 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Numbers: Q01425664-I1, Q01425663-I1 📊 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: NYC permits week ending July 17, 2026: largest new building 112K SF, 8-story, 50 apartments at 103 Empire Boulevard (Crown Heights/Prospect-Lefferts Gardens).
Queens: 82.7K SF, 10-story, 86 apartments at 45/49 Vernon Boulevard; Bronx: two 9-story, 99-unit buildings at Tremont (128.7K SF combined).
Mixed-use pipeline active across outer boroughs; continued residential development momentum in Brooklyn/Bronx/Queens.
stories.summary: NYC's top construction permits for the week ending July 17, 2026, include: (1) a 112,279 SF, eight-story mixed-use project with 50 apartments at 103 Empire Boulevard in Crown Heights/Prospect-Lefferts Gardens; (2) an 82,731 SF, 10-story mixed-use building with 86 apartments at Vernon Boulevard in Queens; and (3) two nine-story apartment buildings with 99 units each in Tremont, Bronx, totaling 128,738 SF. Multiple office alterations were also filed, including a 192,245 SF alteration at Brookfield's 250 Vestry Street (Financial District).
stories.impact: Steady residential pipeline in NYC outer boroughs (Brooklyn, Queens, Bronx) signals continued development momentum despite Manhattan office distress. The permits indicate builders are targeting mixed-use (residential+retail) opportunities in emerging/densifying neighborhoods (Crown Heights, Queens waterfront, Tremont). The continued office alterations (Vestry Street, JPMorgan Chase's Myrtle Avenue) hint at office-to-other conversions. For multifamily developers: outer borough pipeline remains robust; expect continued rent growth and absorption in Queens/Bronx/Brooklyn. Manhattan office weakness has NOT yet cascaded into full-scale office-to-residential conversions at scale, but alterations suggest selective repositioning. Developers should monitor conversion economics as office cap rates diverge from multifamily, creating conversion opportunities in lower Manhattan and Financial District over next 12–18 months.
